Trends:
- Wartime coercion and administrative penetration (1939-45): mobilization, rationing, requisitions, censorship, policing, identity controls. Strengthened the coercive/administrative reach of the state, though under occupation French sovereignty was sharply weakened.
- Suppression and fragmentation of civil society under Vichy and German occupation (1940-44): parties, unions, press, and associations were banned or controlled; Jews, Freemasons, communists, and résistants were persecuted. Weakened autonomous civil society; strengthened authoritarian state power locally.
- Growth of the Resistance and underground civil society (1941-44): networks, maquis, clandestine press, unions, and the Conseil National de la Résistance. Strengthened society’s capacity to organize and later helped legitimate the post-Liberation state.
- Postwar dirigisme and welfare-state construction (1944-48): national planning, nationalizations, social security, stronger ministries, ENA-trained technocracy. Strengthened state capacity; also empowered organized labor through social-insurance administration and collective bargaining.
- Rebirth of mass electoral politics and unions (1944-48): PCF, SFIO, MRP, CGT, CFTC, veterans’ and Resistance groups became powerful. Strengthened civil society and societal checks on the state, but also made the Fourth Republic politically fragmented.
- Economic scarcity and reconstruction pressures (1944-48): housing shortages, rationing until 1949, inflation, black markets, war damage. Weakened state legitimacy in the short run, while justifying stronger state economic management.

Events:
- September 1939: France declares war on Germany and mobilizes. Strengthened state command over society through conscription and emergency powers; constrained civil liberties.
- May-June 1940: German invasion, military collapse, mass civilian exodus, fall of Paris. Severely weakened the French state and disorganized civil society.
- June-July 1940: Armistice with Germany and creation of Vichy after the National Assembly grants full powers to Pétain. Weakened republican state sovereignty; strengthened an authoritarian, collaborationist state; weakened democratic civil society.
- 1940-42: Vichy’s antisemitic laws, repression of parties/unions, and collaboration with German policing. Strengthened coercive state power; gravely weakened pluralist civil society and rights.
- November 1942: Germany occupies the southern “free zone.” Further weakened French state autonomy; increased repression but also pushed more people toward Resistance.
- February 1943: Service du Travail Obligatoire sends French labor to Germany. Strengthened coercive state extraction; weakened state legitimacy; strengthened Resistance recruitment and social opposition.
- May 1943: Conseil National de la Résistance formed. Strengthened organized civil society and created a programmatic bridge to a renewed republican state.
- June-August 1944: Allied landings, Liberation of Paris, and establishment of de Gaulle’s Provisional Government. Restored French state sovereignty; strengthened Resistance-linked civil society.
- April 1944/October 1945: women receive the vote and vote nationally for the first time. Strengthened civil society and democratic participation.
- 1945-46: nationalizations, creation of Sécurité Sociale, and Monnet Planning Commission. Strengthened state economic and welfare capacity; gave unions and social partners institutional influence.
- October 1946: Constitution of the Fourth Republic adopted. Rebuilt republican legality and parliamentary democracy; strengthened civil society representation but created a relatively weak executive.
- 1947-48: major strikes, Communist ministers expelled, CGT split/FO formed, Cold War polarization. Strengthened organized labor’s visibility but fragmented civil society; pressured and destabilized the state.
